sections.title: Keep the queue frontier and the graph layers in view

featuredSetups.label: Layered campus BFS
featuredSetups.description: Use a graph where the next layer fills in cleanly from left to right.

featuredSetups.label: Hub and detours BFS
featuredSetups.description: Start from the center so the queue widens before it narrows again.

pageIntro.whyItMatters: It shows why breadth-first search finds layer-by-layer reachability instead of turning a queue into a memorized rule.
pageIntro.keyTakeaway: Because the oldest discovered node leaves first, breadth-first search clears one depth layer before moving deeper.
pageIntro.definition: Keep the queue frontier, visited count, and graph layers visible together so breadth-first search reads as a layered process instead of a procedure list.

paragraphs: Breadth-first search keeps the frontier organized like a queue. The oldest claimed node gets expanded next, so the search spreads outward in visible layers instead of diving down one branch immediately.

On this bench the graph, the queue frontier, and the visited count stay coupled. That makes the layered shape of BFS honest: nearby nodes get settled first because the queue keeps earlier discoveries ahead of later ones.

keyIdeas: Breadth-first search expands the oldest waiting node first.
On an unweighted graph, breadth-first layers match the fewest-edge distance from the start.
The queue frontier grows when a node discovers new neighbors and shrinks when those waiting nodes get expanded.

commonMisconception.myth: Breadth-first search just means moving around the page in a wide-looking pattern.
correction: The important rule is not the drawing. The important rule is that the oldest claimed node leaves the frontier next.
That queue rule is what makes BFS settle the graph layer by layer.

workedExamples.title: Live worked example
workedExamples.intro: Read the queue frontier directly from the layered-campus bench.

items.title: Read the first BFS layers
items.prompt: On the layered campus graph, what should BFS settle first from A?

steps.label: 1. Read the first layer
steps.template: Starting from A, the first neighbors B and C become the first queue frontier.

steps.label: 2. Keep the queue order honest
steps.template: Because BFS uses a queue frontier, B should leave the frontier before the later discovery C.

steps.label: 3. Read what that means
steps.template: That oldest-first rule makes the search finish the shallow layer before it moves into deeper nodes like D, E, F, and H.

items.resultTemplate: From A, BFS settles the B/C layer before it pushes deeper.
items.interpretationTemplate: The queue frontier is what turns a graph traversal into a layered search rather than a branch-first dive.

applyAction.label: Show layered-campus BFS
applyAction.observationHint: Keep the frontier cue and the visited-history graph visible together so the layer order never turns into a slogan.

miniChallenge.prompt: Build a BFS case where the frontier gets wide before the target is reached.
miniChallenge.prediction: Decide whether you need an edge start or a hub start before you switch presets.
miniChallenge.answer: Use a hub-like start so BFS can claim several same-depth neighbors before it narrows again.
miniChallenge.explanation: Breadth-first search widens fastest when one node has several direct neighbors at the same shallow depth.

challengeMode.title: Challenge mode
challengeMode.intro: Use the graph bench to prove that a wide BFS frontier still comes from queue order, not from a wide-looking drawing.

items.title: Build a wide layered frontier
items.prompt: Start from the hub-and-detours graph at C, keep BFS mode on, and explain why the frontier widens before the target is reached without breaking layer order.
items.successMessage: You built the right BFS scene: a hub start creates a wider shallow frontier, and queue order still keeps that layer ahead of deeper detours.

targets.label: Keep traversal mode set to BFS.

hints: Apply the Hub and detours BFS preset, then watch the frontier cue after C expands.
A wide frontier is still a shallow layer when the queue keeps earlier discoveries ahead of later detours.

equations.label: Layer depth rule
equations.meaning: A newly discovered neighbor sits one edge deeper than the node that found it.

equations.label: Queue frontier update
equations.meaning: Breadth-first search removes the oldest frontier node, then adds its new neighbors to the back.

variableLinks.label: Graph layout
variableLinks.description: Switches among the bounded graph scenes on the same traversal bench.

variableLinks.label: Start node
variableLinks.description: Moves the launch point so the first BFS layer can change without replacing the graph.

variableLinks.label: Target node
variableLinks.description: Marks which node the queue-based search is trying to reach.

variableLinks.label: Traversal mode
variableLinks.description: Keeps the queue-based BFS mode pinned to the same graph bench used for DFS comparisons.

controls.label: Graph
controls.description: Swap among a few bounded graph scenes without leaving the shared bench.

controls.label: Target node
controls.description: Mark the node the search is trying to reach.

controls.label: Traversal mode
controls.description: Compare queue-like breadth-first expansion with stack-like depth-first branching.

presets.label: Layered campus BFS
presets.description: A clean graph where the first frontier is easy to read.

presets.label: Bridge cycle BFS
presets.description: A graph with loops where the queue still settles the shallow side first.

presets.label: Hub and detours BFS
presets.description: A centered start node that widens the frontier quickly.

presets.label: Hub and detours DFS
presets.description: The same detour graph, now branch-first for contrast.

overlays.label: Adjacency cue
overlays.shortDescription: Keep the current node's neighbor list visible.
whatToNotice: The local neighborhood is the input that every BFS layer grows from.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps breadth-first search tied to real graph structure instead of to a slogan about breadth.

overlays.label: Frontier cue
overlays.shortDescription: Show which claimed nodes are waiting next.
whatToNotice: The queue frontier keeps earlier discoveries ahead of later ones.
overlays.whyItMatters: It turns the BFS layer rule into something visible on the bench itself.

overlays.label: Visited cue
overlays.shortDescription: Mark which nodes have already been fully expanded.
whatToNotice: Visited nodes grow outward in the same shallow-to-deep order that the queue enforces.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes the BFS layer pattern readable instead of inferred after the fact.

graphs.label: Visited nodes versus frontier size
graphs.description: Watch how many nodes have already been expanded while the waiting frontier grows or shrinks.

graphs.label: Current depth versus deepest claimed depth
graphs.description: Track how BFS settles shallower depths before later nodes move deeper into the graph.

graphs.label: New discoveries versus repeat skips
graphs.description: Compare fresh BFS discoveries with the repeat edges that visited state prevents from looping back.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Keep the graph and one traversal graph visible together.

items.text: Breadth-first search settles the shallow layer before it lets the graph dive into deeper branches.
A queue frontier often widens first because one shallow node can claim several same-depth neighbors before the queue starts to empty.

predictionMode.title: Predict
predictionMode.intro: Predict the next queue step before you advance the scene.

items.prompt: On the layered-campus BFS run from A, which node should leave the frontier first after A discovers both B and C?

choices.label: B, because BFS keeps the oldest claimed node at the front of the queue.
C, because the newest discovery always goes next.
H, because the target always jumps to the front.

items.explanation: Breadth-first search is queue-based, so B leaves the frontier before the later discovery C.
items.observationHint: Run layered-campus BFS and watch the frontier chips after A expands.

quickTest.title: Quick test
quickTest.intro: Answer from the visible queue and depth pattern, not from memorized acronyms.

questions.prompt: Why does BFS discover shallow nodes before deeper ones on this bench?

choices.label: Because the queue frontier keeps earlier discoveries ahead of later ones.
Because BFS always teleports to the target node.
Because BFS ignores the graph's adjacency.

questions.explanation: The queue frontier is what makes shallow discoveries settle before the search moves deeper.

selectedWrongExplanations.b: BFS still follows graph edges; it does not teleport.
selectedWrongExplanations.c: BFS is built from adjacency rather than ignoring it.

questions.prompt: What is the clearest reason BFS is useful on an unweighted graph?

choices.label: It always uses fewer total edges than the graph actually has.
Its layer order lets the first discovery of a node reflect the fewest-edge distance from the start.
It removes the need for a frontier at all.

questions.explanation: Because BFS settles the graph layer by layer, the first time it reaches a node is already the shallowest edge-distance route.

selectedWrongExplanations.a: BFS may inspect many edges; its usefulness here is the layer-distance guarantee.
selectedWrongExplanations.c: The frontier is essential for BFS order.

questions.prompt: What keeps the BFS frontier behaving like layers instead of one deep branch?

choices.label: The current node is always chosen by largest degree first.
BFS removes all already discovered nodes from the graph itself.
Nodes wait in queue order, so earlier discoveries are expanded before later ones.

questions.explanation: Queue order is what preserves the visible layer-by-layer widening pattern.

selectedWrongExplanations.a: Degree is not the core BFS ordering rule on this bench.
selectedWrongExplanations.b: The graph stays the same; the frontier bookkeeping is what changes.

questions.prompt: Why keep visited state visible during BFS?

choices.label: So BFS can ignore the frontier entirely.
So the search does not keep reopening the same nodes and muddying the layer story.
So the target can move after the search starts.

questions.explanation: Visited state keeps the queue work bounded and stops the traversal from recycling the same nodes.

selectedWrongExplanations.a: Visited helps the frontier stay clean; it does not replace it.
selectedWrongExplanations.c: The target is fixed. The visited set is about bookkeeping the search.

questions.prompt: What is the cleanest difference between the current node and the frontier nodes?

choices.label: They are always the same node set in BFS.
Frontier nodes are already fully processed, but the current node is not.
The current node is being expanded now, while frontier nodes are waiting for their turn.

questions.explanation: BFS separates the node being expanded from the queue of discovered nodes that are still waiting.

selectedWrongExplanations.a: The current node comes out of the frontier and is processed while the rest keep waiting.
selectedWrongExplanations.b: The already fully processed nodes belong to visited history, not the frontier.

paragraphs: The simulation shows one labeled graph with the current node, the queue frontier, and the visited nodes colored differently so the BFS layers stay readable.
A readout card reports the traversal mode, current node, frontier size, visited count, and target, while a cue panel shows the frontier order and the current neighbor list.
One graph tracks visited nodes against frontier size, a second tracks current depth against the deepest claimed depth, and a third compares new discoveries with repeat skips.
Together they show how breadth-first search widens and then settles the graph layer by layer.

seo.title: Breadth-First Search and Layered Frontiers
seo.description: See breadth-first search on one live graph where the queue frontier, depth layers, and visited state stay visible together.

v2.intuition: Keep the queue frontier, visited count, and graph layers visible together so BFS feels like a layered process instead of a memorized procedure list.
v2.whyItMatters: Breadth-first search is often the first shortest-path intuition students meet on unweighted graphs, so the queue needs to feel like a visible layer rule rather than a black-box data structure.

guidedSteps.title: Read the first layer
guidedSteps.goal: Use one clean BFS run to see the first queue frontier fill with same-depth neighbors.
guidedSteps.doThis: Start with the layered-campus-bfs preset and watch which nodes enter the frontier right after A.
guidedSteps.notice: The first frontier is the B/C layer, and both nodes wait before the search moves deeper.
guidedSteps.explain: BFS begins with the immediate neighbors of the start because those are the first nodes one edge away.

guidedSteps.title: Keep the oldest node first
guidedSteps.goal: See why the queue frontier clears older discoveries before newer ones.
guidedSteps.doThis: Run the same layered graph, then focus on which frontier node leaves first after the first layer fills.
guidedSteps.notice: The node that was claimed earlier leaves the frontier before the later discovery.
guidedSteps.explain: A queue is first-in, first-out, so BFS preserves the order in which frontier nodes were discovered. That is what keeps the search layered.

guidedSteps.title: Widen the queue on a hub
guidedSteps.goal: Use a broader graph to show that BFS still respects shallow layers even when the frontier gets wide.
guidedSteps.doThis: Apply the hub-detours-bfs preset and watch the frontier widen before the target is reached.
guidedSteps.notice: The queue can get wide, but BFS still settles the shallow layer before it moves to deeper nodes.
guidedSteps.explain: On an unweighted graph, BFS layers match fewest-edge distance from the start because the queue exhausts each depth before opening the next one.

guidedSteps.title: Prove the wide frontier
guidedSteps.goal: Turn the hub example into a challenge: make the frontier wide while keeping BFS layered.
guidedSteps.doThis: Open the challenge setup, keep the hub-detours graph in BFS mode, and use the frontier cue to explain why the queue stays layered.
guidedSteps.notice: The frontier can hold several same-depth nodes at once, but the oldest waiting node still leaves first.
guidedSteps.explain: A wide frontier does not contradict BFS. It is exactly what happens when several shallow neighbors have been discovered and are waiting in queue order.

learned: BFS fills the first frontier with same-depth neighbors before it goes deeper.
The queue frontier clears older discoveries first, which is what makes the search layered.
On an unweighted graph, BFS layers line up with fewest-edge distance from the start.
wrapUp.misconception: Breadth-first search just means moving around the page in a wide-looking pattern.

nextConcepts.title: Depth-First Search and Backtracking Paths
nextConcepts.reasonLabel: Contrast queue order with stack order

wrapUp.freePlayPrompt: Build one BFS case with a narrow frontier and one with a wide frontier, then explain why both still stay layered.
